summary: I was born in Alexandria, Egypt, on September 17, 1974. Growing up there, I experienced a life where women were protected, but also restricted; I couldn't go out without my father or another family member. Alexandria was the place of my childhood, but I felt confined and unable to see the world for myself. It was from Alexandria that I eventually left to start a new life in America after marrying Mahmoud Hassan.

In Egypt, where I was born and raised, women had certain protections but also strict limitations on their freedom. I wasn't allowed to go out alone and had to be accompanied by my father or another family member. Life in Egypt was very different from what I later experienced in America. Although I felt restricted there, it was my home until I married an American citizen and immigrated at the age of 19.

I moved to the United States of America when I married Mahmoud Hassan, an American citizen, at just 19 years old. Coming to America was a huge change; I didn't know what to expect and found many things hard and different. My husband had lost the strong family values I was used to, and he spent time partying and drinking, which went against my beliefs. Learning English and connecting with the Egyptian community helped me find my place, and over time, I began to live the American dream despite the challenges.

Islam has always been an important part of my life and beliefs. When I came to America, I noticed that my husband Mahmoud's drinking was against our Islamic faith, which made me uncomfortable. My values, shaped by Islam, influenced how I viewed his behavior and the lifestyle differences between us. Holding onto my Islamic beliefs helped me navigate the challenges I faced in a new country and culture.
